analyst_observations: The skill achieves 100% pass rate on all process assertions (plan, manifest, log, verification gates). The baseline achieves 0% on these — it never creates tracking artifacts.
Both configurations achieve identical code outcomes: modules extracted, tests passing. The difference is entirely in the process discipline.
Eval 2 (mid-session resume) is non-discriminating: the baseline also read the manifest and resumed correctly. When an existing plan is present, the skill adds less marginal value.
Eval 3 baseline kept core.js as a facade rather than deleting it — a common pattern where agents take the safe route. The skill's plan explicitly called for deletion.
The skill costs ~2x more tokens and ~2.4x more time. This is the cost of verification gates and manifest updates. Whether that tradeoff is worth it depends on risk tolerance and codebase size.

The verification-failure eval didn't actually trigger a failure — the agents were smart enough to use re-export facades during migration. A harder test fixture would be needed to test the failure-handling path.
